title: Looking to validate an idea for trading research tool
author: Hot_Style5572
selftext:
The flow:  
1. Define a chart fragment/draw own chart.  
2. Set Similarity Score threshold.  
3. See historical charts with similarity score above that threshold and analyze if a repeatable trading rules in these situations bring positive PnL.

The image shows the selected setup on the left and all outcomes of the matched charts on the right.

With transaction parameters fixed for all these outcomes, you'd get:  
\- 62% win rate  
\- 1:1 risk to reward ratio  
\- total profit of 3.33% (pre-leverage) on 61 trades.

Can work on any type of pattern, price action, candle behavior and be combined with other indicators.

Looking for your thoughts and opinions.

comments.author: _BeeSnack_
comments.body:
I made an app like this

And all you do is flip a coin

  
Not as flashy as your UI, but similar odds calculator

replies.author: Hot_Style5572
replies.body:
It gets better than a coinflip if you add risk of overfitting and get enough samples to achieve statistically significant results.

It's also not supposed to be used as a blind signal provider, but rather a base for research to gather enough reliable data to trust a strategy - as opposed to running manual backtesting.

comments.author: _Tychonic_
comments.body:
Take a step back and consider the fundamental mechanism. You’re basically suggesting that you can predict future movement based on similar past movement, without any consideration for what drives the movement. You’re saying you can predict the rest of the sentence based on the first two words. You’re saying breaking news, macroeconomic factors, public sentiment, earnings events, etc do not need to be factored in.
There is a reason the right side of your example is 100% noise.

replies.author: Hot_Style5572
replies.body:
Thanks a lot for the feedback!

If you incorporate volume and/or volatility instead as measurements of the regime the market is in, it should provide more information than the existence of some fundamental event. Do you agree?

As for the example, at which point would you conclude that the outcomes are actually significant? Is it a certain win rate on a big enough sample? Some other stat?

replies.author: silphotographer
replies.body:
I am sympathetic about this sentiment and it's not wrong but I think it's kinda misleading thing to say given OP's context. There are no strategies that can predict 100%.  AFAIK there are no strategies that can handle breaking news, volatile public sentiments, ever-so-changing macroeconomic factors, etc. I think (I could be wrong as I don't have access to his source code) is the thesis that history may not repeat exactly but it rhymes. Past outcomes do not necessarily predict the future, but the fact that stock market tend to have patterns big picture wise is not the wildest statement (ex. classic retail DCA on big too big to fail index funds). Even if his theory is not the strongest if backtesting shows profitability and if it can be replicated more or less in forward testing and live account with managed capital risk for experiment (ex. get a cheap prop firm account and size down the trading position to establish real proof of concept), then who cares if his rational is not ironclad?  
  
Not condemning or condoning OP's strategy as we are nowhere close to that yet whether he's right or wrong, but to say what he has is 100% noise seem a bit of a hyperbole without concrete proof.

comments.author: EventHorizonbyGA
comments.body:
This is built-in to Godel Terminal already.  If "similarity score" actually finds similar stock setups in the historical record.

The stock market goes up 54% of the time. A 62% win rate likely isn't enough to account of spread effects and slippage.

When you model you need to not take the midpoint of the spread but the ask (if you are buying) or the bid (if you are selling) to determine you win/rate and return.

replies.author: Hot_Style5572
replies.body:
Noted that, sir :)  
In this specific example the exact results are irrelevant, it only serves to showcase the idea. You're right though, that including spread at the very least is a must.  
  
We're actually looking for more people to share their thoughts, if you'd be down to join our group of testers, we would appreciate that a lot! Sharing link where you can see an overview of the features and play around with the Similarity Score in the Sandbox section: [https://stratosphere-trading.com](https://stratosphere-trading.com)

replies.author: EventHorizonbyGA
replies.body: You want other people to test your idea?  If you aren't willing to put your own money at-risk than you already know your idea doesn't work.

replies.author: Hot_Style5572
replies.body:
It's not about testing a trading strategy or risking anyone's capital.  
We're using similarity score do conduct our own research already and what we're trying to validate is whether other people see value in it as a source of reliable backtesting data - not as a signal provider or anything like that.

Trying to find out the best way for the tool to provide as much value for traders in their strategy exploration as possible. Does that make sense?

replies.author: EventHorizonbyGA
replies.body: No. It doesn't. Best of luck though.

comments.author: Hot_Style5572
comments.body:
For those looking to test out the similarity algorithm and find out more, here's the link:  
[https://stratosphere-trading.com/](https://stratosphere-trading.com/)

You can join the waitlist there and the testing group if you'd like.

Appreciate you all!

comments.author: darkpplord
comments.body: What’s the filtered sample size relative to total sample size? And have u stratified data based on volatility? Cuz what ive found is that a lot of “signals” are simple outcomes of vol clustering, almost all of them lose their directional edge once u factor in vol distribution

replies.author: Hot_Style5572
replies.body:
The total sample was 11k records (candles), but we skip sections when an outcome is matched to avoid overlapping results.

Great insight about the volatility - we haven't implemented that yet, but we for sure will add that to the backlog.

What's important here is that it's not supposed to be a signal generator, but a research tool to validate strategies based on deterministic similarity algorithm - as opposed to subjective judgement in manual testing.  
That being said, I am no trying to say "just blindly trust the predictions". The point is to find chart fragments that have the same characteristics based on a number of conditions (similarity score), rather than only indicators.

Going back to the volatility part - this would be a great next "ingredient" for the similarity score then.

comments.author: More-Competition-377
comments.body: Ooooh, that sounds like such a cool idea for a tool! Using pattern matching like that for backtesting is smart. Is this something you built yourself? 🤔

replies.author: Hot_Style5572
replies.body:
Yes sir, you can check out the details here:  
[https://stratosphere-trading.com/](https://stratosphere-trading.com/)
